topics.title: Spring Security — JWT, Filters & OAuth2
topics.description: Spring Security is a filter chain that intercepts every HTTP request before it reaches your controllers. Authentication answers 'who are you?' (login → token). Authorization answers 'what can you do?' (@PreAuthorize, hasRole). JWT (JSON Web Token) is the stateless token format: base64url(header).base64url(payload).signature — self-contained, no server-side session storage needed. OAuth2 enables third-party login (Google, GitHub) by delegating authentication to an external provider.
topics.story: Think of Spring Security as airport security. Every passenger (HTTP request) must pass through checkpoints (filter chain). First: check boarding pass (authentication filter — is your JWT valid?). Second: check if your ticket allows entry to business class (authorization — hasRole('ADMIN')). JWT is like a biometric boarding pass — it contains your identity and permissions embedded, so any airport (server instance) can verify it without calling the booking system. OAuth2 is like using your Aadhar card (Google) to verify identity — you trust Google to confirm 'yes, this is really Aditya'.
topics.analogy: Filter chain = airport security lanes. UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter = ID check at first desk. JwtAuthFilter = biometric scan. SecurityContext = the wristband you wear after passing security (holds your identity for this request). @PreAuthorize = the VIP lounge door check. JWT header.payload.signature = boarding pass with name, seat, and tamper-proof barcode. OAuth2 = using your passport (Google account) instead of a custom ID.
topics.memoryAnchor: FCJA security chain — Filter chain intercepts all requests → JWT verified in custom filter → Authentication object set in SecurityContext → Authorization rules checked before Controller runs. JWT three dots: header.payload.signature. VERIFY mnemonic: Validate signature (HMAC-SHA256) → check Expiry → extract Claims → Trust the principal. OAuth2 delegation: User logs in at Provider (Google) → Provider sends token → Your app trusts the token, never sees the password. Security maturity ladder: BSJO — Basic auth → Session-based → JWT stateless → OAuth2 delegated. BCrypt is the only acceptable password encoder. SecurityFilterChain bean replaces WebSecurityConfigurerAdapter (deprecated in Spring 5.7+).
topics.firstPrinciples: HTTP is stateless — every request is independent. Traditional sessions store auth state server-side (scalability problem). JWT embeds auth state in the token — server only needs the secret to verify. The signature prevents tampering: if payload changes, signature validation fails. Stateless authentication enables horizontal scaling: any server instance can validate any token.
topics.bruteForce: Session-based auth: login → server creates HttpSession → stores in memory/Redis → returns session cookie → every request looks up session in store → O(1) DB lookup per request but requires centralized session store for clustering. Sticky sessions as workaround break load balancing.
topics.optimizedApproach: JWT stateless auth: login → server generates JWT signed with secret → client stores token → every request sends token in Authorization header → server validates signature locally (no DB) → extracts claims → sets SecurityContext. Scales horizontally. Downside: can't revoke individual tokens (use short expiry + refresh token blacklist for revocation).
topics.whenToUse: JWT: stateless APIs, microservices, mobile clients. Sessions: traditional web apps with server-side rendering, when you need instant token revocation. OAuth2: third-party login (don't build your own auth for Google/GitHub login). Basic Auth: machine-to-machine, internal APIs. API keys: simple integrations where user context isn't needed. Always use HTTPS — tokens in transit are meaningless without TLS.

examples.title: JWT Generation, Validation & Spring Filter
examples.explanation: JwtService.generate() builds a signed JWT with userId, roles, and expiry. validate() verifies the signature and returns claims — if the token was tampered or expired, an exception is thrown. JwtAuthFilter extends OncePerRequestFilter (runs exactly once per request). It extracts the Bearer token, validates it, creates a UsernamePasswordAuthenticationToken with roles, and sets it in SecurityContextHolder — making the user 'authenticated' for this request.
examples.realWorldUse: This is exactly the pattern in DevLearner's JwtAuthFilter. JwtService signs/verifies tokens. After filter sets authentication, @PreAuthorize('hasRole(ADMIN)') checks the roles in SecurityContext. Token stored in localStorage, sent as 'Authorization: Bearer ...' header. Refresh token flow: short-lived access token (15min) + long-lived refresh token (7 days) in httpOnly cookie.

examples.title: SecurityConfig — Route Rules, CORS, Password Encoding
examples.explanation: SessionCreationPolicy.STATELESS tells Spring not to create HTTP sessions — pure JWT mode. addFilterBefore registers JwtAuthFilter in the chain before the default auth filter. CSRF disabled for stateless APIs (CSRF attacks require cookies; JWT in Authorization header is safe). BCryptPasswordEncoder is the only acceptable password hasher — bcrypt includes salt + iterations. @EnableMethodSecurity enables @PreAuthorize on service/controller methods.
examples.realWorldUse: DevLearner SecurityConfig matches this pattern exactly. /api/admin/** → ROLE_ADMIN only. /api/auth/login → permitAll. Everything else → authenticated. CORS configured for React dev server. BCrypt: passwordEncoder.encode(rawPassword) on register; passwordEncoder.matches(raw, hash) on login.

problems.title: JWT Decode Without Library
problems.hint1: Split token on '.' → parts[1] is the payload.
problems.hint2: Base64URL uses '-' instead of '+' and '_' instead of '/'. Add '=' padding to make length multiple of 4.
problems.hint3: Use Base64.getDecoder().decode() after restoring standard base64.
problems.sampleOutput: JWT validated; user authenticated; request proceeds

problems.title: BCrypt Password Verification
problems.hint3: Store hash in Map<String, String>. Show two hashes for same password are different.
problems.sampleOutput: 403 Forbidden — AccessDeniedException thrown before method executes

problems.title: Role-Based Access Simulation
problems.hint1: Define a Map<String, List<String>> with role → allowed endpoint prefixes.
problems.hint2: Check if the endpoint starts with any allowed prefix for the role.
problems.hint3: ADMIN has a wildcard '/' that matches everything.
problems.sampleInput: User clicks 'Sign in with Google'; Google redirects back with code
problems.sampleOutput: OAuth2 flow completes; user created/found in DB; JWT issued

problems.title: JWT Claims Validation
problems.description:
Implement a JWT validator that checks all three validity conditions: (1) signature is correct, (2) token is not expired, and (3) required claims are present. Return a detailed validation result.

**Production rule:** Never trust a JWT without validating ALL three. An expired but valid-signature token must be rejected. A non-expired token with a bad signature must be rejected. A structurally valid token missing 'sub' is rejected.

**Key insight:** JWT libraries like jjwt throw `ExpiredJwtException`, `SignatureException`, and `MalformedJwtException` separately. Your exception handler should return 401 with a meaningful message so clients know whether to refresh (expired) or re-login (bad signature).
problems.pattern: JWT validation, claims inspection, exception handling
problems.hint1: Parse the token in three phases: decode header/payload (no signature check), then verify signature, then check expiry.
problems.hint2: Use a flag-based approach: validSignature, notExpired, hasRequiredClaims — return all failures.
problems.hint3: Simulate a token as 'header.payload.signature' where signature = payload.hashCode() as a simple stand-in for HMAC.
problems.sampleOutput: Correct validation result with specific error codes per failure type

problems.title: CSRF Token Simulation
problems.description:
Implement a CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) protection mechanism. A CSRF attack tricks a logged-in user's browser into making unauthorized requests to your site using their cookie. The fix: include a secret CSRF token in every state-changing request (POST/PUT/DELETE) that the attacker's site can't read.

**Pattern:** Server generates a random token, stores it in session, embeds it in every HTML form or returns it in a header. On each state-changing request, server verifies the submitted token matches the session token.

**Key insight:** JWT APIs don't need CSRF because JWT is in Authorization header (not cookies) — XSS is the threat instead. Session-cookie-based apps DO need CSRF. Spring Security enables CSRF by default for stateful apps and disables it for stateless JWT apps.
problems.pattern: CSRF protection, token validation, stateful security
problems.hint1: Session stores a random UUID per session. The request must include a matching token.
problems.hint2: POST/PUT/DELETE require CSRF token; GET/OPTIONS are safe methods — no check needed.
problems.hint3: Constant-time comparison: use MessageDigest.isEqual() to prevent timing attacks on token comparison.
problems.testCases:
Token generated: true
GET: true
POST valid: true
POST invalid: false
POST missing: false
problems.sampleInput: Four requests: GET, POST with valid token, POST with wrong token, POST with null
problems.sampleOutput: GET allowed; valid token allowed; attacker token rejected; missing token rejected

problems.title: BCrypt Cost Factor Benchmark
problems.description:
Measure how BCrypt's cost factor (work factor) affects hashing time. BCrypt is deliberately slow — each time the cost increases by 1, hashing takes 2x longer. This makes brute-force attacks exponentially harder while keeping legitimate logins acceptably fast.

**Rule of thumb:** Target 100-300ms hash time at production load. For a server doing 10 logins/second, cost=12 (≈250ms) is typical. If hardware gets faster, increase cost without changing the algorithm.

**Key insight:** Password hashing must be slow (BCrypt, Argon2, scrypt). MD5/SHA-256 are fast by design — a GPU can compute billions/second, making brute-force trivial. BCrypt's slowness is a feature.
problems.pattern: BCrypt cost factor, security vs performance trade-off
problems.hint1: Use BCryptPasswordEncoder(strength) where strength is the cost (4-31 range).
problems.hint2: Measure with System.currentTimeMillis() before and after encode().
problems.hint3: Print cost=X, time=Yms for costs 4, 8, 10, 12 and show 2x pattern.
problems.testCases:
cost=4  time≈1ms
cost=8  time≈10ms
cost=10 time≈40ms
cost=12 time≈160ms
Note: cost+1 ≈ 2x slower (exponential work factor)
problems.sampleInput: BCrypt with cost factors 4, 8, 10, 12 encoding same password
problems.sampleOutput: Timing shows exponential growth — each +4 cost adds roughly 16x time

problems.title: Security Context Propagation
problems.description:
Simulate how Spring Security propagates the authenticated user (SecurityContext) across async threads. By default, `SecurityContextHolder` uses `ThreadLocal` — the async thread has a different ThreadLocal and loses the authentication. Solution: use `DelegatingSecurityContextRunnable` or configure Spring Security's async support.

**Real-world problem:** `@Async` methods in Spring don't inherit the caller's SecurityContext by default. If your async method calls `SecurityContextHolder.getContext().getAuthentication()` it gets null — leading to 403 errors or NPE in logs.

**Key insight:** DevLearner's `@Async` submission processing needs the user's identity for audit logging — SecurityContext must be propagated to the task thread.
problems.hint1: SecurityContextHolder.getContext() returns the context from the CURRENT thread's ThreadLocal.
problems.hint2: To propagate: capture context = SecurityContextHolder.getContext() in caller thread, then set it in async thread.
problems.hint3: Use a wrapper Runnable that sets the context before running and clears it after (DelegatingSecurityContextRunnable pattern).
problems.testCases:
Main thread user: alice
Async without wrap: null
Async with wrap: alice
problems.sampleInput: async thread without and with SecurityContext propagation
problems.sampleOutput: Without wrap: null (ThreadLocal lost); with wrap: alice (context preserved)
